request.question: Ronnie Dunn and Jenna Sanz-Agero were both formerly part of bands in which they each acted in what role?

title: Mama Don't Get Dressed Up for Nothing
Ronnie Dunn
Kix Brooks
If You See Him/If You See Her
Only in America (Brooks & Dunn song)
Jenna Sanz-Agero
Peace, Love, and Country Music
Ronnie Dunn (album)
Polyrically Uncorrect
Bleed Red
sentences: "Mama Don't Get Dressed Up For Nothing" is a song co-written and recorded by American country music duo Brooks & Dunn.
It was released in September 1996 as the third single from their CD "Borderline", and their eighteenth single overall.
The song received moderate success on the US Country chart, where it peaked at number 13, while it was a number 8 hit in Canada.

It was the first single the duo released that missed the US country top 10, breaking a streak of 17 straight top 10 entries; the next eight singles Brooks and Dunn released after this would also make the country top 10.
This is the fourth single to feature Kix Brooks on lead vocals instead of Ronnie Dunn.
Kix and Ronnie wrote this song, along with Don Cook.
Ronnie Gene Dunn (born June 1, 1953) is an American country music singer-songwriter and record executive.
In 2011, Dunn began working as a solo artist following the breakup of Brooks & Dunn.
He released his self-titled debut album for Arista Nashville on June 7, 2011, reaching the Top 10 with its lead-off single "Bleed Red".
In 2013, after leaving Arista Nashville in 2012, Dunn founded Little Will-E Records.
On April 8, 2014, Ronnie Dunn released his second solo album, "Peace, Love, and Country Music" through his own Little Will-E Records.

Leon Eric "Kix" Brooks III (born May 12, 1955) is an American country music artist, actor, and film producer best known for being one half of the duo Brooks & Dunn and host of radio's "American Country Countdown".
Prior to the duo's foundation, he was a singer and songwriter, charting twice on Hot Country Songs and releasing an album for Capitol Records.
Brooks and Ronnie Dunn comprised Brooks & Dunn for 20 years, with both members beginning solo careers.
Brooks's solo career after Brooks & Dunn has included the album "New to This Town".

"If You See Him/If You See Her" is a song written by Terry McBride, Jennifer Kimball and Tommy Lee James, and recorded by American country music artist Reba McEntire, along with the duo Brooks & Dunn.
It served as the title track to each artist's respective 1998 albums ("If You See Him" for Reba, and "If You See Her" for Brooks & Dunn), both released on June 2 of that year.
The song was concurrently promoted and distributed by both artists' labels: MCA Nashville and Arista Nashville, then the respective labels for McEntire and Brooks & Dunn.
It is the only single to feature both Kix Brooks & Ronnie Dunn on vocals.
"Only in America" is a song recorded by American country music duo Brooks & Dunn.
It was released in June 2001 as the second single from the album "Steers & Stripes".
Kix Brooks, one-half of the duo, co-wrote the song with Don Cook and Ronnie Rogers, although it features Ronnie Dunn on lead vocals.

"Only in America" was also the second of three consecutive Number One hits from that album, reaching its peak on the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles & Tracks (now Hot Country Songs) charts for the week of October 27, 2001.
Jenna Sanz-Agero is the former lead singer of the female rock group Vixen, and a former president of Media 8.
She lives and works in Los Angeles, California.
Peace, Love, and Country Music is the second solo studio album by country music artist Ronnie Dunn.
The album was released on April 8, 2014 via Dunn's own record label Little Will-E Records.
"Peace, Love, and Country Music" is Dunn's first solo release since 2011's "Ronnie Dunn" released on Arista Nashville.
Ronnie Dunn is the debut solo studio album from country music artist Ronnie Dunn.
The album was released on June 7, 2011, via Arista Nashville.
The album is Dunn's first release of music in nearly 25 years; he released three singles in the 1980s without issuing an album.
Polyrically Uncorrect is the tenth studio album, and fourteenth album release overall, by American country music parodist Cledus T. Judd.
It was released on June 30, 2009 via E1 Music.
It includes the singles "Waitin' on Obama", "Garth Must Be Busy" and "(If I Had) Kellie Pickler's Boobs".
The album includes guest vocals from Ashton Shepherd, Ronnie Dunn, Jamey Johnson, Terry Eldredge (of The Grascals), Colt Ford and Daryle Singletary.
Chris Neal of "Country Weekly" gave the album three stars out of five, citing the Ronnie Dunn collaboration as a standout track.
"Bleed Red" is a song written by Tommy Lee James and Andrew Dorff, and recorded by American country music singer Ronnie Dunn.
The song is Dunn's first solo release after Brooks & Dunn disbanded in 2010.
"Bleed Red" was released to country radio on January 31, 2011.
It is intended to be the lead-off single from Dunn's first solo album, "Ronnie Dunn".

He had previously released three singles: "It's Written All Over Your Face", "She Put the Sad in All His Songs", and "Jessie"; with the first two singles charting to number 59 on the U.S. "Billboard" Hot Country Singles & Tracks (now Hot Country Songs) chart.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: Ronnie Dunn and Jenna Sanz-Agero were both formerly part of bands in which they each acted in what role?
hop1_docs: Jenna Sanz-Agero | Jenna Sanz-Agero is the former lead singer of the female rock group Vixen, and a former president of Media 8.  She lives and works in Los Angeles, California.

Rita Sanz-Agero | Rita Sanz-Agero (born November 8, 1991 in Guatemala City) is a female modern pentathlete from Guatemala.  At age sixteen, Sanz-Agero became the youngest modern pentathlete to compete at the 2008 Summer Olympics in Beijing, where she finished thirty-fourth in the women's event, with a score of 4,844 points.
María Isabel Sanz-Agero | María Isabel Sanz-Agero (born November 20, 1965) is a Guatemalan modern pentathlete.  She placed 31st in the women's individual event at the 2004 Summer Olympics.

Brooks & Dunn | Brooks & Dunn is an American country music duo consisting of Kix Brooks and Ronnie Dunn, both vocalists and songwriters.  The duo was founded in 1990 through the suggestion of Tim DuBois.  Before the foundation, both members were solo recording artists.  Brooks wrote number one singles for John Conlee, Nitty Gritty Dirt Band, and Highway 101; both he and Dunn also charted two solo singles a piece in the 1980s, with Brooks also releasing an album for Capitol Records in 1989.  However, they began hitting the charts together in 1991.

If You See Him/If You See Her | "If You See Him/If You See Her" is a song written by Terry McBride, Jennifer Kimball and Tommy Lee James, and recorded by American country music artist Reba McEntire, along with the duo Brooks & Dunn.  It served as the title track to each artist's respective 1998 albums ("If You See Him" for Reba, and "If You See Her" for Brooks & Dunn), both released on June 2 of that year.  The song was concurrently promoted and distributed by both artists' labels: MCA Nashville and Arista Nashville, then the respective labels for McEntire and Brooks & Dunn.  It is the only single to feature both Kix Brooks & Ronnie Dunn on vocals.

That's What She Gets for Loving Me | "That's What She Gets for Loving Me" is a song written by Ronnie Dunn and Terry McBride, and recorded by American country music duo Brooks & Dunn.  It was released in February 2004 as the third and final single from their album "Red Dirt Road".  It peaked at number 6 in the United States.

Ashley Monroe | Ashley Lauren Monroe is an American country music singer-songwriter.  She has released two solo singles on the U.S. "Billboard" Hot Country Songs chart.  The singles "Satisfied" and "I Don't Want To" (which featured Brooks & Dunn singer Ronnie Dunn) reached No. 43 and No. 37, respectively.  Both singles were intended to be released on Monroe's debut album, "Satisfied", in 2007, but the album went unreleased.  Monroe left Columbia Records' roster in late 2007, and "Satisfied" was finally released on May 19, 2009.  In June 2011, Monroe, Miranda Lambert and Angaleena Presley formed a band called Pistol Annies.  Monroe's second studio album, "Like a Rose", was released on March 5, 2013 followed by her third album "The Blade", released on July 24, 2015.
hop1_titles: If You See Him/If You See Her
That's What She Gets for Loving Me
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- Jenna Sanz-Agero — [1]: "former lead singer of the female rock group Vixen,"
- Ronnie Dunn — [4]: "both vocalists and songwriters."

Ambiguities:
- None. Jenna Sanz-Agero maps to the Vixen lead singer role in [1]; Ronnie Dunn maps to vocalist (and songwriter) in Brooks & Dunn in [4].

Bridge / missing fact:
- No missing facts: the passages explicitly state the band roles needed to answer the question.

Retrieval direction:
- "Jenna Sanz-Agero" (page)
- "Brooks & Dunn" (page)
- "Vixen (band)" (page)
- "Ronnie Dunn" (page)

Final conclusion: both acted as singers — Jenna Sanz-Agero was the lead singer of Vixen and Ronnie Dunn was a vocalist (of Brooks & Dunn).
hop2_docs: Jenna Sanz-Agero | Jenna Sanz-Agero is the former lead singer of the female rock group Vixen, and a former president of Media 8.  She lives and works in Los Angeles, California.

Rita Sanz-Agero | Rita Sanz-Agero (born November 8, 1991 in Guatemala City) is a female modern pentathlete from Guatemala.  At age sixteen, Sanz-Agero became the youngest modern pentathlete to compete at the 2008 Summer Olympics in Beijing, where she finished thirty-fourth in the women's event, with a score of 4,844 points.
María Isabel Sanz-Agero | María Isabel Sanz-Agero (born November 20, 1965) is a Guatemalan modern pentathlete.  She placed 31st in the women's individual event at the 2004 Summer Olympics.

Ronnie Dunn | Ronnie Gene Dunn (born June 1, 1953) is an American country music singer-songwriter and record executive.  In 2011, Dunn began working as a solo artist following the breakup of Brooks & Dunn.  He released his self-titled debut album for Arista Nashville on June 7, 2011, reaching the Top 10 with its lead-off single "Bleed Red".  In 2013, after leaving Arista Nashville in 2012, Dunn founded Little Will-E Records.  On April 8, 2014, Ronnie Dunn released his second solo album, "Peace, Love, and Country Music" through his own Little Will-E Records.

Bleed Red | "Bleed Red" is a song written by Tommy Lee James and Andrew Dorff, and recorded by American country music singer Ronnie Dunn.  The song is Dunn's first solo release after Brooks & Dunn disbanded in 2010.  "Bleed Red" was released to country radio on January 31, 2011.  It is intended to be the lead-off single from Dunn's first solo album, "Ronnie Dunn".  He had previously released three singles: "It's Written All Over Your Face", "She Put the Sad in All His Songs", and "Jessie"; with the first two singles charting to number 59 on the U.S. "Billboard" Hot Country Singles & Tracks (now Hot Country Songs) chart.

Ronnie Dunn (album) | Ronnie Dunn is the debut solo studio album from country music artist Ronnie Dunn.  The album was released on June 7, 2011, via Arista Nashville.  The album is Dunn's first release of music in nearly 25 years; he released three singles in the 1980s without issuing an album.

Cost of Livin' | "Cost of Livin'" is a song co-written and recorded by American country music singer Ronnie Dunn.  It was released in June 2011 as the second single from his self-titled album, and his second release following the split of the duo Brooks & Dunn.  Dunn wrote this song with Phillip Coleman.
response.summary_2: Jenna Sanz-Agero was the lead singer of the rock group Vixen, and Ronnie Dunn was a vocalist (singer) in the duo Brooks & Dunn.
